content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
May 25,
1994
Mary A. Horton
Mayor, City of Pinole
2986 Higuera Ave.
Pinole,
Ca 94564
Re:
Your Request for Confirmation
-
of Telephone Advice
Our File No.
1—94—166
Dear Ms. Horton:
You have requested confirmation of telephone advice provided
to you regarding the campaign disclosure provisions of the
Political Reform Act
(the “Act”) .
Your letter states a general
question and therefore we are treating it as one for informal
assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c).
2
QUESTION
May you control and make contributions from personal funds to
a primarily formed ballot measure committee called “Taxpayers,
Homeowners and Law Enforcement for a Safer Pinole Committee”
(THLESPC)?
1
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000,
.
All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114;
2 Cal.
Code of Regs.
Sections
18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800
•
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Horton, Mary
File No.
A—94—166
Page
2
CONCLUSION
There is nothing in the Act which prohibits you from making
contributions to, participating in or controlling a primarily
formed ballot measure committee.
FACTS
You are the Mayor of the City of Pinole,
and you may appear
for re—election on the November 8,
1994 ballot.
However, you have
not as yet formed a controlled committee in support of your re
election and there are currently no active committees which you
control for your own candidacy.
You have not decided whether you
are going to seek re-election in 1994.
THLESPC has not filed a
Statement of Organization
(Form 410) but will do so shortly.
It
is also my understanding that THLESPC is formed primarily to
support the passage of a local advisory ballot measure on the June
ballot and you wish to participate in directing its activities and
you also wish to make contributions to it from personal funds.
Th advisory measure will recommend or request that the City
Council of Pinole take a certain action.
You have indicated that
some of your colleagues on the Council have indicated that your
involvement with the ballot measure is inappropriate in light of
your position on the City Council and that such activity
constitutes a conflict of interest for you.
ANALYSIS
An individual, including a candidate or officeholder,
who
receives contributions totaling $1,000 or more in a calendar year
qualifies as a committee under the Act.
(Section 82013.)
When a
candidate or offieholder has significant influence over the
actions and decisions of a committee, it is defined as a
“controlled committee”.
(Section 82016.)
The Act requires that a
candidate may have only one campaign bank account for the receipt
of all contributions and expenditures made in connection with his
or her election to a particular office.
(Section 85201.)
Therefore,
a candidate may establish only one controlled committee
and bank account for each office the candidate seeks.
(Evans
Advice Letter,
No.
1—91-031.)
However, there is nothing in the Act which prevents an
officeholder from making contributions from personal funds or
directing the activities of a primarily formed ballot measure
committee.
If THLESPC qualifies as a committee as defined in
Section 82013 by collecting or spending $1,000 or more in a
calendar year, it must show on its Statement of Organization
(Form
410) that it is controlled by you and that it is primarily formed
to support the ballot measure.
You may control THLESPC as well as
a separate committee in support of your candidacy so long as
THIJESPC does not, in addition to its ballot measure activity, also
make expenditures on your behalf as a candidate.


Horton,
Mary
File No.
A—94—166
Page
3
Furthermore, you will have no conflict of interest under the
Act by controlling a ballot measure committee.
Section 87100,
which sets forth the Act’s conflict of interest rules, provides
that:
...no public official at any level of state or lócal
government shall make, participate in making or in any
way attempt to use his official position to influence a
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to
know he has a financial interest.
When an official controls a ballot measure committee, he or
she does not “make” or “participate in making” a government
decision.
(See generally Regulation 18700.)
Furthermore, under
Regulation 18700.1(b) (2),
communications with the general public
or press do not constitute using one’s official position to
/
influence a government decision.
Therefore, actions in
controlling a ballot measure committee are not within the coverage
of the Act’s conflict of interest provisions.
If you have additional questions, please contact me at
(916)
322—5662.
Sincerely,
By:
Lt4
P. Imberi
Reform Consultant
